perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information disclosed to Receiving Party hereunder shall: a. not be copied or distributed, disclosed, or disseminated in any way or form by the Receiving Party to any third party without the written permission of MyPoints.com first obtained; b. be treated by the Receiving Party with the same degree of care to avoid disclosure to any third party as is used with respect to the Receiving Party's own proprietary and confidential information of like importance; c. remain the property of the MyPoints.com, and shall be returned by the Receiving Party to MyPoints.com (along with all copies thereof) promptly upon its receipt of a request from MyPoints.com to do so; d. not be used by Receiving Party for any purpose other than as specified herein or otherwise approved by MyPoints.com in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information disclosed to Receiving Party hereunder shall: a. not be copied or distributed, disclosed, or disseminated in any way or form by the Receiving Party to any third party without the written permission of MyPoints.com first obtained, unless required by law; b. be treated by the Receiving Party with reasonable care to avoid disclosure to any third party; c. remain the property of the MyPoints.com, and shall be returned by the Receiving Party to MyPoints.com (along with all copies thereof) promptly upon its receipt of a request from MyPoints.com to do so; d. not be used by Receiving Party for any purpose other than as specified herein or otherwise approved by MyPoints.com in writing.
perturbation.explanation: The changes weaken the restrictions on handling confidential information. Introducing the 'unless required by law' exception to the non-disclosure clause (a) and changing 'the same degree of care' to 'reasonable care' (b) creates a conflict. The original stringent conditions implied a higher standard of confidentiality, whereas the altered terms provide loopholes and subjective assessments. This contradicts the original intent of strict confidentiality and raises uncertainty about the Receiving Party's obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: It is understood and agree that monetary damages will not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party, and that MyPoints.com shall be entitled to specific performance and/or injunctive relief as a remedy for any such breach of this Agreement, but said remedies shall be in addition to all other remedies available at law or in equity. It is further agreed that this Agreement is made for the benefit of MyPoints.com, and that no failure or delay by MyPoints.com to enforce its rights hereunder shall operate as a waiver of any right, power or privilege under this Agreement, nor shall any single or partial exercise thereof preclude any other or further exercise thereof.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces exceptions ('unless required by law') and weakens the standard of care ('reasonable care') for handling confidential information. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which asserts that monetary damages are insufficient for a breach and that MyPoints.com is entitled to specific performance and injunctive relief. The original text implies a very high standard of confidentiality, justifying such remedies. The changed text undermines this by allowing disclosures required by law and using a more subjective 'reasonable care' standard, making it harder to prove a breach and justify the extraordinary remedies outlined in the contradicted text, creating a significant in-text contradiction and uncertainty about enforcement.

perturbation.original_text: The obligations set forth in Paragraph 3 above shall not apply to any information which: a. is already in the public domain at the time of disclosure to the Receiving Party or becomes available to the public through no breach of this Agreement by the Receiving Party; b. was lawfully in the Receiving Party's possession prior to receipt from the MyPoints.com; c. is disclosed to Receiving Party by a third party with the right to do so. For the purposes of this Paragraph 4, information shall not be deemed to be in the public domain merely because any part of said information is embodied in general disclosures or because individual features, components or combinations thereof are now, or become, known to the public, provided, however, that the obligations of Paragraph 3 hereof shall not apply to any such part of said information.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations set forth in Paragraph 3 above shall not apply to any information which: a. is already in the public domain at the time of disclosure to the Receiving Party or becomes available to the public; b. was in the Receiving Party's possession prior to receipt from the MyPoints.com; c. is disclosed to Receiving Party by a third party. For the purposes of this Paragraph 4, information shall not be deemed to be in the public domain merely because any part of said information is embodied in general disclosures or because individual features, components or combinations thereof are now, or become, known to the public, provided, however, that the obligations of Paragraph 3 hereof shall not apply to any such part of said information.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause provides exceptions to the confidentiality obligations, specifying that the exceptions only apply if the public domain availability wasn't due to a breach of the agreement and if the third party had the right to disclose. Removing these qualifications broadens the exceptions and introduces conflict. Now, even if information enters the public domain due to the Receiving Party's breach, it's no longer protected, contradicting the original intent of preventing breaches. This creates a loophole by negating protections in Section 3.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidential Information disclosed to Receiving Party hereunder shall: a. not be copied or distributed, disclosed, or disseminated in any way or form by the Receiving Party to any third party without the written permission of MyPoints.com first obtained; b. be treated by the Receiving Party with the same degree of care to avoid disclosure to any third party as is used with respect to the Receiving Party's own proprietary and confidential information of like importance; c. remain the property of the MyPoints.com, and shall be returned by the Receiving Party to MyPoints.com (along with all copies thereof) promptly upon its receipt of a request from MyPoints.com to do so; d. not be used by Receiving Party for any purpose other than as specified herein or otherwise approved by MyPoints.com in writing.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the qualifications regarding breach of agreement and third-party rights from the exceptions to confidentiality obligations in Paragraph 4. This directly contradicts the confidentiality obligations outlined in the Contradicted Text, specifically section a, which prohibits the Receiving Party from disclosing confidential information to any third party without written permission. The original text protected against the Receiving Party causing the information to enter the public domain. Now, the changed text allows for information to enter the public domain because of the Receiving Party's breach, and the confidentiality obligations would no longer apply, directly conflicting with the obligations to protect Confidential Information set in the contradicted text. This follows the definition of an "Omissions – In Text Contradiction".

perturbation.original_text: Unless otherwise mutually agreed in writing, the Receiving Party's obligations with respect to each item of Confidential Information shall terminate five (5) years from the date of the receipt thereof by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless otherwise mutually agreed in writing, the Receiving Party's obligations with respect to each item of Confidential Information shall terminate five (5) years from the date of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The change affects when the confidentiality obligations terminate. Originally, the five-year period started from the receipt of each item of Confidential Information, meaning the obligation period could vary for different pieces of information. By changing it to 'five years from the date of this agreement,' all obligations now expire on the same date, regardless of when the information was received. This conflicts with the concept of protecting specific items of information individually and creates uncertainty when the actual date of receipt of different items differ, which directly influence the compliance with the terms of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Agreement shall bind Receiving Party and those taking under it with regard to all Confidential Information disclosed to, or obtained by, Receiving Party hereunder. For the purposes of this Agreement Confidential Information shall include all information disclosed, directly or indirectly, through any means of communication or observation, by MyPoints.com to or for the benefit of Receiving Party, that relates to or is derived from MyPoints.com's technical, business, strategic, marketing or creative affairs, or to any other matter that the Receiving Party is advised or has reason to know is the confidential or proprietary information of MyPoints.com. Any material provided by MyPoints.com to Receiving Party which is clearly designated "Confidential" (or other similar legend) will be presumed to be Confidential Information. The absence of any such legend, however, will not preclude the same from being deemed Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text specified that confidentiality obligations terminated five years from the date of receipt of each item of confidential information, allowing for staggered termination dates. The changed text alters this to five years from the date of the agreement, which means all confidentiality obligations would terminate on the same date. This contradicts the contradicted text, which emphasizes that the agreement binds the receiving party with regard to 'all Confidential Information disclosed to, or obtained by, Receiving Party hereunder', implying individual consideration of each piece of confidential information. The change creates an in-text contradiction because the obligation termination date is now uniform regardless of when the Receiving Party obtained the information, undermining the clause binding the Receiving Party 'with regard to all Confidential Information disclosed' separately. Therefore, the change introduces ambiguity and creates a contradiction as the actual receipt date of the confidential information influences compliance but is no longer considered.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that Receiving Party becomes legally compelled to disclose any of the Confidential Information, Receiving Party shall provide MyPoints.com with prompt notice so that it may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy and/or waive compliance with the provisions of this Agreement. In the event MyPoints.com is unable to obtain such protective order or other appropriate remedy, only that portion of the Confidential Information which has been deemed by a written opinion of counsel to be legally required to be furnished, shall be disclosed, and Receiving Party will cooperate with the MyPoints.com to obtain a protective order or other reliable assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded the Confidential Information so disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that Receiving Party believes it is legally compelled to disclose any of the Confidential Information, Receiving Party shall make a reasonable effort to provide MyPoints.com with notice. In the event MyPoints.com is unable to obtain such protective order or other appropriate remedy, all Confidential Information shall be disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: The change drastically alters the procedure for legally compelled disclosure. By changing 'becomes legally compelled' to 'believes it is legally compelled' and removing the requirement for a written opinion of counsel, the bar for disclosure is lowered significantly. The original clause allowed only the legally required portion to be disclosed, while the modified clause mandates disclosure of all Confidential Information if a protective order cannot be obtained. This conflicts with the core principle of the agreement - protecting confidentiality. In particular, this weakens the obligations around Confidential Information treatment.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidential Information disclosed to Receiving Party hereunder shall: a. not be copied or distributed, disclosed, or disseminated in any way or form by the Receiving Party to any third party without the written permission of MyPoints.com first obtained; b. be treated by the Receiving Party with the same degree of care to avoid disclosure to any third party as is used with respect to the Receiving Party's own proprietary and confidential information of like importance; c. remain the property of the MyPoints.com, and shall be returned by the Receiving Party to MyPoints.com (along with all copies thereof) promptly upon its receipt of a request from MyPoints.com to do so; d. not be used by Receiving Party for any purpose other than as specified herein or otherwise approved by MyPoints.com in writing.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly weakens the confidentiality obligations. The original text requires 'prompt notice' and allows disclosure of only the 'legally required portion' with a written opinion of counsel. The changed text reduces the notice requirement to a 'reasonable effort', broadens the disclosure to 'all Confidential Information' if a protective order is unobtainable, and removes the requirement for written opinion. This directly contradicts the 'Contradicted Text,' which mandates that Confidential Information 'not be copied or distributed, disclosed, or disseminated in any way or form' without written permission, be treated with the same degree of care as the Receiving Party's own confidential information, and remain the property of MyPoints.com. The modified clause creates a significant loophole for disclosure, directly conflicting with the absolute restriction on disclosure in the Contradicted Text.
